text: Hey, I am Dr. Rajasekhar C. Jaka demonstrating the operative technique of robotic transthoracic esophageal mobilization and complete mediastinal lymphadenctomy. He is a young patient with squamous cell carcinoma of lower esophagus post neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Patient is in prone position with single lung ventilation.

The surgeon utilizes two robotic working ports, with a hot shear in the right hand and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand. The ports are positioned 7 cm away from the posterior midline, with the upper port in the third intercostal space and the lower port in the eighth intercostal space. The camera is placed just below the tip of the scapula, and the assistant port is introduced inferior and anterior to the camera port. A 30-degree down camera is used, and the patient cart is docked from the left shoulder side.
The surgeon inspects the esophagus for adhesions and metastasis, then begins dissection in the infra-azygous region between the pericardium and the anterior aspect of the esophagus.

The surgeon divides the pleura and carefully dissects lymph nodes and lymphatics towards the esophagus. At least fifteen lymph nodes are removed for adequate staging. The video shows the lymph node being taken towards the specimen, with the tumor location in the esophagus divided into three zones: upper, middle, and lower.

The surgeon performs a mediastinal lymphadenectomy, dissecting infracarinal lymph nodes. The procedure progresses to extended mediastinal lymphadenectomy, involving dissection around the right recurrent laryngeal nerve and right paratracheal lymph nodes. Finally, complete mediastinal lymphadenectomy is achieved by dissecting additional lymph nodes around the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, left paratracheal, and subiotic areas.

The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament near the inferior pulmonary vein. The fat around the pericardium is retracted towards the specimen. The pulmonary artery is visible as the assistant provides traction on the pericardium, separating fat, lymphatics, and lymph nodes away from the heart and pericardium.

The surgeon dissects down to the right crest near the diaphragmatic hiatus, removing lymph nodes and fat near the pulmonary artery. The dissection progresses towards the subcarinal area, focusing on lymph node removal.

The surgeon divides both vagus nerves below the carina and performs subcarinal lymph node dissection, noting minor bleeding due to vascularity. The esophagus is dissected away from the left bronchus, encountering the first bronchial artery. The pleura is divided between the azygous vein and the posterior part of the esophagus, with caution to avoid injuring the thoracic duct visible in the surrounding fat. If injured, the thoracic duct is identified and ligated above the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon performs an en bloc resection, removing the primary tumor along with the pericardium, thoracic duct, azygous vein, intercostal vessels, and bilateral pleura overlying the tumor. Surrounding fat, lymphatics, and lymph nodes are carefully taken towards the specimen, ensuring the thoracic duct remains intact.

The surgeon divides the left side mediastinal pleura and moves it towards the specimen to achieve a proper margin. The left lung is visible through the created window. The dissection proceeds down to the diaphragmatic hiatus on the left side. After completing the dissection in the infra-azygous part, the surgeon plans to move to the supra-azygous part.

The surgeon identifies tracheal ring impressions and begins by dividing the pleura near the azygous vein. Care is taken to avoid using active energy sources near the tracheoesophageal membrane, instead applying traction to separate the pleura from the tracheal membrane. The arch of the azygous vein is freed, and two Chenamom hemlock clips are placed on either side before the vein is divided into two segments.

The surgeon dissects the area between the aorta and pulmonary vessel, moving towards the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and left paratracheal area. The assistant provides traction on the tracheal ring, allowing access to the ventral and dorsal sides of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. Sharp dissection is performed to remove a chain of lymph nodes present in this region.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the thoracic duct while dissecting a large lymphatic structure. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, curving around the reticule arch, and a branch leading to the esophagus is being divided.

The surgeon introduces umbilical tape to provide traction on the dissected esophagus, allowing the assistant to assist in completing the dissection on the left side. The subcarinal lymph node dissection is performed last to minimize bleeding during the procedure. The assistant removes the lymph nodes, and the dissection is completed around the aorta, carina, and left side of the trachea. The umbilical tape is then removed.
